---
title: "Tenth Summer School on Statistical Methods for Linguistics and Psychology"
subtitle: "Advanced methods in frequentist statistics with Julia"
author:
  - "Phillip Alday"
  - "Reinhold Kliegl"
---

This site provides materials for the **Advanced frequentist methods** stream of the [Summer School on Statistical Methods](https://vasishth.github.io/SMLP2026/) to be held at the University of Potsdam, 24--28 August 2026.
The stream focuses on fitting, evaluating, and interpreting linear and generalized linear mixed-effects models using the [Julia programming language](https://julialang.org) and the [MixedModels.jl](https://juliastats.org/MixedModels.jl/stable/) package.

# How to use these materials

Every page is a complete, runnable analysis. You can engage with them two ways:

- **Read along.** The rendered website shows all code and its output, so you can follow the
  reasoning without running anything. Good for a first pass.
- **Run locally.** Clone the repository and execute the code yourself — change things, break
  things, and do the [exercises](#exercises-and-self-check) at the bottom of each core page.
  This is where the learning happens. Start with [Setup: installing the tools](getting-started/setup.qmd).

Most pages in the core path open with a **Learning objectives** box (what you'll be able to
do afterwards) and a **Before you start** box (which pages to read first and which datasets
are used), so you can navigate and pace yourself without an instructor in the room.

::: {.callout-tip title="I just need to look something up"}
The [Reference & Appendix](reference/useful_packages.qmd) section is lookup material, not a
lesson sequence: a tour of [useful Julia packages](reference/useful_packages.qmd), how to
[save and load data with Arrow](reference/arrow.qmd), and
[package management for reproducible environments](reference/pkg.qmd). Dip in as needed.
:::

# Suggested path

1. **[Start Here](getting-started/setup.qmd)** — install the tools, meet the instructors, and skim the [dataset catalog](getting-started/datasets.qmd).
2. **Foundations: Linear Mixed Models** — fit and interpret your first mixed model ([sleepstudy](lmm-intro/sleepstudy.qmd)) and learn to recognize [singular fits](lmm-intro/singularity.qmd).
3. **Seeing Your Model** — [shrinkage plots](visualization/shrinkageplot.qmd) and [multi-panel graphics](visualization/AoGPlots.qmd); diagnostic tools you'll reuse everywhere.
4. **Building Models** — random-effects [specification](model-specification/partial_within.qmd) and selection for [designed](model-specification/mrk17.qmd) and [large-scale](model-specification/largescaledesigned.qmd) experiments.
5. **Contrast Coding & Transformations** — make your coefficients answer the questions you actually care about ([transformations](contrasts/transformation.qmd), contrast schemes).
6. **Inference & Uncertainty** — [bootstrap](inference/bootstrap.qmd), [profiling](inference/profiling.qmd), and [power simulation](inference/power_simulation.qmd).
7. **Generalized Linear Mixed Models** — [extending to non-Gaussian responses](glmm/glmm.qmd).
8. **Capstone Worked Examples** — full analyses that pull the pieces together: the [Emotikon project](examples/emotikon/fggk21.qmd) and the [visual-attention experiments](examples/visual-attention/kwdyz11.qmd).

# Dependency map

The core path is roughly linear, but not strictly: once you have the Foundations, several
sections can be read in any order. The arrows show what each section assumes you've already
seen.

```{mermaid}
flowchart TD
    F["Foundations:<br/>Linear Mixed Models"]
    V["Seeing Your Model:<br/>Visualization & Diagnostics"]
    B["Building Models:<br/>Specification & Random Effects"]
    C["Contrast Coding<br/>& Transformations"]
    I["Inference &<br/>Uncertainty"]
    G["Generalized Linear<br/>Mixed Models"]
    X["Capstone<br/>Worked Examples"]

    F --> V
    F --> B
    F --> C
    F --> G
    B --> I
    V --> X
    C --> X
    I --> X
```

# Datasets at a glance

A handful of datasets recur across the course. The [dataset catalog](getting-started/datasets.qmd)
describes each one — its design, variables, citation, and how to load it. Here is where each
appears:

| Dataset | Used in |
|:--------|:--------|
| `sleepstudy` | [sleepstudy](lmm-intro/sleepstudy.qmd), [singularity](lmm-intro/singularity.qmd), [transformations](contrasts/transformation.qmd), [speed metrics](contrasts/sleepstudy_speed.qmd), [bootstrap](inference/bootstrap.qmd), [GLMM](glmm/glmm.qmd) |
| `kb07` | [shrinkage plots](visualization/shrinkageplot.qmd), [bootstrap](inference/bootstrap.qmd), [profiling](inference/profiling.qmd), [kb07](inference/kb07.qmd), [power simulation](inference/power_simulation.qmd) |
| `fggk21` (Emotikon) | [contrast coding](contrasts/contrasts_fggk21.qmd), [AoG plots](visualization/AoGPlots.qmd), [Emotikon capstone](examples/emotikon/fggk21.qmd), [transform check](examples/emotikon/check_emotikon_transform.qmd) |
| `kwdyz11` | [contrast coding](contrasts/contrasts_kwdyz11.qmd), [visual-attention capstone](examples/visual-attention/kwdyz11.qmd) |
| `kkl15` | [replication & model reduction](examples/visual-attention/kkl15.qmd) |
| `mrk17` | [model specification](model-specification/mrk17.qmd) |

# Getting started

New here? Head to **[Setup: installing the tools](getting-started/setup.qmd)** to get git,
Julia, and Quarto working before the course, then read **[About](getting-started/about.qmd)**
for the course framing and instructor bios.

---

*This page was rendered from git revision {{< git-rev short=true >}} using Quarto {{< version >}} and Julia {{< julia-version >}}.*
